content:
```json
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Train a large medical LLM on a unified biomedical/clinical corpus combining PubMed abstracts, clinical notes, medical textbooks, and USMLE-style licensing exam questions, using autoregressive language modeling for generation plus instruction prompt tuning for clinical QA and alignment.",
  "niche_genome": "Clinical AI for biomedical text generation, medical question answering, clinical note summarization, and USMLE-style licensing exam support, where general-purpose LLMs often lack domain-specific knowledge and alignment.",
  "observation_genome": "The model should outperform PubMedQA-only and Med-PaLM-style instruction-tuned models on clinical QA benchmarks, while generating coherent medical summaries, discharge notes, and exam-style explanations.",
  "limitation_genome": "The model may still struggle with rare diseases, ambiguous clinical cases, and factual updates outside the training window; alignment and safety remain open challenges.",
  "delta_genome": "Compared with BioGPT, the proposal adds clinical notes, medical textbooks, and exam-style QA; compared with Med-PaLM, it adds domain-specific generation and a broader biomedical/clinical pretraining objective.",
  "claim_genome": "A unified biomedical/clinical LLM pre-trained on PubMed, clinical notes, medical textbooks, and licensing exams can achieve both strong generation and clinical QA performance while being more aligned with real-world medical practice."
}
```
prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Clinical AI / Medical Large Language Models who understands how scientific ideas evolve.
Below is an important research lineage in this area, where each paper evolves from its predecessors:

**[2022] BioGPT: Generative Pre-trained Transformer for Biomedical Text Generation and Mining**
Key Contribution: BioGPT: first domain-specific generative pre-trained Transformer for biomedical text, pre-trained on 15 million PubMed abstracts; achieves 78.2% on PubMedQA and state-of-the-art on biomedical relation extraction tasks, demonstrating the value of domain-specific generative pretraining for biomedicine.
Target Problem: General-purpose language models lack deep biomedical knowledge; existing biomedical NLP models (BioBERT, PubMedBERT) are encoder-only and cannot generate text, limiting their utility for tasks like biomedical text generation, question answering, and document summarization.
Core Method: GPT-2 medium architecture (347M parameters, 24 layers, 1024 hidden units) pre-trained from scratch on 15 million PubMed abstracts; uses autoregressive language modeling objective specifically on biomedical literature to learn domain-specific knowledge and generation capabilities.
Observed Evidence: BioGPT achieves 78.2% accuracy on PubMedQA, 44.98% F1 on BC5CDR relation extraction, and strong performance on KD-DTI and DDI extraction tasks; demonstrates that domain-specific generative pretraining yields superior biomedical NLP performance compared to general-purpose models.
Remaining Limitation: Relatively small model (347M parameters) compared to general LLMs; pre-trained only on PubMed abstracts without clinical notes or medical textbooks; cannot handle clinical question answering or medical licensing exams; no instruction tuning or alignment for interactive use.
Expected Result/Claim: BioGPT: first domain-specific generative pre-trained Transformer for biomedical text, pre-trained on 15 million PubMed abstracts; achieves 78.2% on PubMedQA and state-of-the-art on biomedical relation extraction tasks, demonstrating the value of domain-specific generative pretraining for biomedicine.

  ↓

**[2023] Large language models encode clinical knowledge**
Key Contribution: Med-PaLM: first LLM to surpass the passing threshold on USMLE-style questions (67.6% on MedQA); introduces MultiMedQA benchmark and instruction prompt tuning for medical domain alignment.
Target Problem: General-purpose LLMs perform poorly on medical question answering due to lack of clinical knowledge alignment; no comprehensive benchmark existed to evaluate LLMs across diverse medical QA tasks from professional, research, and consumer perspectives.
Core Method: Flan-PaLM (540B parameter instruction-tuned LLM) combined with instruction prompt tuning — a parameter-efficient method that learns soft prompts from a few medical exemplars to align the model to the clinical domain without full fine-tuning.
Observed Evidence: Flan-PaLM achieves 67.6% on MedQA (USMLE), surpassing prior state-of-the-art by 17%; Med-PaLM (with prompt tuning) generates answers that approach clinician quality on open-ended questions but still fall short on factuality and reasoning axes.
Remaining Limitation: Text-only model cannot interpret medical images (radiology, pathology, dermatology); single-turn QA format does not capture real clinical dialogue; answers sometimes contain factual inaccuracies and incomplete reasoning chains; closed-source model.
Expected Result/Claim: Med-PaLM: first LLM to surpass the passing threshold on USMLE-style questions (67.6% on MedQA); introduces MultiMedQA benchmark and instruction prompt tuning for medical domain alignment.

**[2023] Capabilities of GPT-4 on Medical Challenge Problems**
Key Contribution: First systematic evaluation of GPT-4 on medical challenge problems; GPT-4 exceeds the USMLE passing score by over 20 points without any specialized prompt crafting, outperforming both GPT-3.5 and domain-fine-tuned models like Med-PaLM, demonstrating that scale alone can encode substantial clinical knowledge.
Target Problem: Med-PaLM showed that specialized prompt tuning of large models could achieve passing-level medical performance, but it remained unclear whether general-purpose frontier models at sufficient scale could encode clinical knowledge without any medical-specific training or prompt engineering.
Core Method: Zero-shot and few-shot evaluation of GPT-4 (general-purpose LLM) on USMLE practice exams and the MultiMedQA benchmark suite without any medical domain fine-tuning or specialized prompt crafting; systematic comparison against GPT-3.5 and medical-domain models.
Observed Evidence: GPT-4 exceeds USMLE passing score by over 20 points; significantly outperforms GPT-3.5 and Med-PaLM (a model specifically fine-tuned for medicine); demonstrates much better calibration than GPT-3.5 in predicting answer correctness; shows emergent clinical reasoning without medical training.
Remaining Limitation: Evaluated only on multiple-choice benchmarks, not open-ended clinical reasoning or dialogue; closed-source commercial model with unknown training data composition; cannot be adapted or fine-tuned by the research community; no multimodal medical capabilities assessed.
Expected Result/Claim: First systematic evaluation of GPT-4 on medical challenge problems; GPT-4 exceeds the USMLE passing score by over 20 points without any specialized prompt crafting, outperforming both GPT-3.5 and domain-fine-tuned models like Med-PaLM, demonstrating that scale alone can encode substantial clinical knowledge.

  Change Summary: GPT-4's medical evaluation directly competes with Med-PaLM in the same target problem of medical QA benchmarks. While Med-PaLM relies on specialized medical prompt tuning, GPT-4 achieves superior performance through sheer scale and general pretraining alone, without any medical-specific adaptation. This parallel route for the same target problem demonstrates that scale can substitute for specialization, pressuring...

**[2023] PMC-LLaMA: Towards Building Open-source Language Models for Medicine**
Key Contribution: PMC-LLaMA: first open-source medical LLM built by fine-tuning LLaMA on 4.8M biomedical papers and 30K medical textbooks with instruction tuning on 202M tokens; 13B-parameter model surpasses ChatGPT on medical QA benchmarks, democratizing access to medical AI.
Target Problem: Med-PaLM and GPT-4 demonstrate strong medical capabilities but are closed-source, preventing community adaptation, safety auditing, and deployment in privacy-sensitive healthcare settings; no high-quality open-source medical LLM existed that could compete with proprietary models.
Core Method: Domain-adaptive pretraining of LLaMA (7B/13B) on 4.8 million PubMed Central papers and 30K medical textbooks for knowledge injection; followed by instruction tuning on a curated dataset of 202M tokens comprising medical QA, reasoning chains, and conversational dialogues for alignment.
Observed Evidence: PMC-LLaMA-13B surpasses ChatGPT on multiple medical QA benchmarks; ablation studies demonstrate the effectiveness of each component (continued pretraining, instruction tuning, data curation); establishes the open-source medical LLM paradigm adopted by subsequent work.
Remaining Limitation: Significantly smaller than proprietary models (13B vs. hundreds of billions); English-only with limited multilingual medical capability; evaluated on benchmarks rather than real clinical settings; knowledge limited to training data cutoff without retrieval augmentation.
Expected Result/Claim: PMC-LLaMA: first open-source medical LLM built by fine-tuning LLaMA on 4.8M biomedical papers and 30K medical textbooks with instruction tuning on 202M tokens; 13B-parameter model surpasses ChatGPT on medical QA benchmarks, democratizing access to medical AI.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: Capabilities of GPT-4 on Medical Challenge Problems)
  Change Summary: PMC-LLaMA speciates from the closed-source Med-PaLM lineage into an entirely different ecological target problem: open-source medical AI. While Med-PaLM uses proprietary PaLM with prompt tuning, PMC-LLaMA fine-tunes the open-source LLaMA on biomedical literature and medical instructions, creating a reproducible, community-adaptable species. This main-method replacement reflects a fundamental divergence in how medi...

**[2023] A Study of Generative Large Language Model for Medical Research and Healthcare**
Key Contribution: GatorTronGPT: 20B-parameter clinical generative LLM trained on 277 billion words including 82 billion words of real clinical notes from 2 million patients; demonstrates that clinical-note-trained LLMs can generate synthetic clinical text indistinguishable from human-written notes by physicians, and synthetic NLP models outperform real-data models.
Target Problem: Existing medical LLMs (BioGPT, Med-PaLM) were trained primarily on biomedical literature (PubMed) rather than real clinical notes from electronic health records; clinical language has distinct characteristics (abbreviations, structured formats, patient-specific context) that literature-trained models cannot capture.
Core Method: GPT-3 architecture scaled to 20 billion parameters; trained from scratch on 277 billion words combining 82 billion words of de-identified clinical text from University of Florida Health (126 departments, 2M patients) with 195 billion words of general English text; evaluated on biomedical NLP tasks and physician Turing tests.
Observed Evidence: GatorTronGPT-generated clinical notes pass physician Turing tests with no significant difference in linguistic readability (p=0.22) or clinical relevance (p=0.91); synthetic NLP models trained on GatorTronGPT-generated text outperform models trained on real clinical text; improves biomedical relation extraction and QA benchmarks.
Remaining Limitation: Trained on data from a single healthcare system (UF Health), potentially limiting generalizability across institutions; 20B parameters still smaller than frontier models; does not handle medical images or multimodal clinical data; requires de-identification infrastructure limiting reproducibility.
Expected Result/Claim: GatorTronGPT: 20B-parameter clinical generative LLM trained on 277 billion words including 82 billion words of real clinical notes from 2 million patients; demonstrates that clinical-note-trained LLMs can generate synthetic clinical text indistinguishable from human-written notes by physicians, and synthetic NLP models outperform real-data models.

While PMC-LLaMA was trained on published papers and textbooks, GatorTronGPT adapts the medical LLM paradigm to real clinical EHR data — 82 billion words of clinical notes from actual patient encounters. This radiation into the clinical documentation target problem produces a model that can genera...

**[2023] MEDITRON-70B: Scaling Medical Pretraining for Large Language Models**
Key Contribution: Meditron-70B: largest open-source medical LLM at the time, extending LLaMA-2 70B with domain-adaptive pretraining on 48.1B tokens from PubMed, clinical guidelines, and medical papers; achieves 6% absolute gain over best open-source baseline and outperforms GPT-3.5 and Med-PaLM on medical benchmarks.
Target Problem: PMC-LLaMA and similar open-source medical LLMs were limited to 13B parameters, significantly underperforming larger proprietary models; the gap between open-source and closed-source medical AI hindered equitable access to medical AI, especially in low-resource healthcare settings.
Core Method: Domain-adaptive pretraining of LLaMA-2 (7B and 70B) on GAP-Replay corpus: 48.1 billion tokens from curated PubMed articles, PubMed Central papers, and internationally recognized clinical practice guidelines, combined with 400M tokens of general-domain data for knowledge retention; systematic investigation of pretraining data composition and scale.
Observed Evidence: Meditron-70B achieves 6% absolute performance gain over best public baseline in its parameter class; outperforms GPT-3.5 and Med-PaLM; within 5% of GPT-4 and 10% of Med-PaLM-2; comprehensive ablation demonstrates the importance of data curation, clinical guidelines, and scale for medical LLM performance.
Remaining Limitation: Text-only model with no multimodal capability; performance still lags behind GPT-4 and Med-PaLM-2 on most benchmarks; evaluated primarily on English medical QA; does not support clinical dialogue or conversational diagnostic interaction; no integration with clinical workflows.
Expected Result/Claim: Meditron-70B: largest open-source medical LLM at the time, extending LLaMA-2 70B with domain-adaptive pretraining on 48.1B tokens from PubMed, clinical guidelines, and medical papers; achieves 6% absolute gain over best open-source baseline and outperforms GPT-3.5 and Med-PaLM on medical benchmarks.

**[2023] Towards Expert-Level Medical Question Answering with Large Language Models**
Key Contribution: Med-PaLM 2: achieves 86.5% on MedQA (expert-level performance) via PaLM 2 base model improvements, ensemble refinement prompting, and medical domain fine-tuning; reaches physician-level quality on 8 of 9 evaluation axes.
Target Problem: Med-PaLM's 67.6% accuracy on MedQA remained below expert physician performance (~87%); human evaluation revealed gaps in factuality, reasoning completeness, and potential for harm compared to clinician-generated answers.
Core Method: PaLM 2 base model with improved pretraining; ensemble refinement — a novel prompting strategy that generates multiple candidate answers, then synthesizes them into a refined final response; combined with medical domain fine-tuning on curated clinical datasets.
Observed Evidence: Med-PaLM 2 achieves 86.5% on MedQA, approaching expert physician level; physician evaluation rates it as equivalent to physicians on 8 of 9 axes; performance gains on MedMCQA, PubMedQA, and MMLU clinical topics; 19% absolute improvement over Med-PaLM.
Remaining Limitation: Still text-only with no multimodal capability; evaluated primarily on multiple-choice benchmarks rather than open-ended clinical scenarios; ensemble refinement adds inference cost; does not engage in multi-turn diagnostic dialogue.
Expected Result/Claim: Med-PaLM 2: achieves 86.5% on MedQA (expert-level performance) via PaLM 2 base model improvements, ensemble refinement prompting, and medical domain fine-tuning; reaches physician-level quality on 8 of 9 evaluation axes.

**[2024] Capabilities of Gemini Models in Medicine**
Key Contribution: Med-Gemini: family of multimodal medical models built on Gemini that integrate text, images, and web search; achieves state-of-the-art on 10 of 14 medical benchmarks and surpasses GPT-4V by 44.5% on multimodal tasks.
Target Problem: Med-PaLM 2 achieves expert-level text QA but cannot process medical images (X-rays, pathology slides, dermatology photos) or leverage real-time clinical knowledge from the web; clinical decision-making inherently requires multimodal reasoning.
Core Method: Gemini multimodal foundation model fine-tuned for medicine with custom medical encoders for novel modalities (e.g., genomics, ECG); integrates native web search for real-time medical knowledge retrieval; long-context reasoning over extended clinical records.
Observed Evidence: Med-Gemini achieves state-of-the-art on 10 of 14 medical benchmarks; surpasses GPT-4V by 44.5% average relative margin on 7 multimodal benchmarks including NEJM Image Challenges; demonstrates emergent clinical reasoning capabilities with web search grounding.
Remaining Limitation: Evaluated on benchmarks rather than real-world clinical deployments; closed-source commercial model limiting reproducibility; multimodal integration still processes each modality somewhat independently rather than true cross-modal fusion; no real-time patient interaction capability.
Expected Result/Claim: Med-Gemini: family of multimodal medical models built on Gemini that integrate text, images, and web search; achieves state-of-the-art on 10 of 14 medical benchmarks and surpasses GPT-4V by 44.5% on multimodal tasks.

**[2025] Towards conversational diagnostic artificial intelligence**
Key Contribution: AMIE (Articulate Medical Intelligence Explorer): LLM-based conversational diagnostic AI optimized for multi-turn clinical dialogue; outperforms primary care physicians on 30 of 32 specialist evaluation axes and 25 of 26 patient-actor axes in a randomized crossover study.
Target Problem: Med-Gemini and Med-PaLM 2 excel at single-turn QA benchmarks but real clinical practice requires multi-turn diagnostic dialogue — history taking, differential diagnosis generation, and shared decision-making through conversation; no prior AI system had been systematically optimized and evaluated for this.
Core Method: Self-play based simulated dialogue environment for large-scale training on diverse clinical scenarios; automated feedback mechanisms for iterative self-improvement; dialogue-specific fine-tuning on clinical communication skills including empathy, thoroughness, and information gathering.
Observed Evidence: In a randomized double-blind crossover study with 149 clinical scenarios and 20 PCPs, AMIE was rated superior on 30 of 32 specialist axes and 25 of 26 patient-actor axes; demonstrates better diagnostic accuracy, information gathering, and communication quality than board-certified physicians in text-based consultations.
Remaining Limitation: Evaluation conducted in simulated text-based consultations with actor patients, not real clinical encounters; does not handle physical examination or procedural guidance; text-only format excludes the multimodal reality of clinical practice; no prospective clinical validation.
Expected Result/Claim: AMIE (Articulate Medical Intelligence Explorer): LLM-based conversational diagnostic AI optimized for multi-turn clinical dialogue; outperforms primary care physicians on 30 of 32 specialist evaluation axes and 25 of 26 patient-actor axes in a randomized crossover study.

**[2026] A prospective clinical feasibility study of a conversational diagnostic AI in an ambulatory primary care clinic**
Key Contribution: First prospective clinical feasibility study of conversational diagnostic AI (AMIE) deployed with 100 real patients in ambulatory primary care; achieves 90% diagnostic accuracy with no safety interventions required, demonstrating clinical viability.
Target Problem: AMIE demonstrated superiority over PCPs in simulated consultations, but the critical gap between simulated evaluation and real-world clinical deployment remained unaddressed; no prior study had prospectively tested a conversational diagnostic AI with actual patients in a clinical setting.
Core Method: Prospective single-center feasibility study at Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center; 100 adult patients complete pre-visit AMIE text-chat interactions up to 5 days before appointments; real-time human safety monitoring; blinded comparison of AMIE vs. PCP differential diagnoses and management plans; 8-week post-encounter chart review for ground truth.
Observed Evidence: AMIE differential diagnosis includes the final diagnosis in 90% of cases (75% top-3 accuracy); blinded assessment shows similar overall quality to PCP diagnoses (p=0.6); patient satisfaction is high and attitudes toward AI improve significantly (p<0.001); no safety interventions needed during any consultation.
Remaining Limitation: Single-center study with 100 patients limits generalizability; PCPs outperform AMIE on practicality (p=0.003) and cost-effectiveness (p=0.004) of management plans; text-only interaction misses physical examination findings; no integration with electronic health records or clinical decision support systems.
Expected Result/Claim: First prospective clinical feasibility study of conversational diagnostic AI (AMIE) deployed with 100 real patients in ambulatory primary care; achieves 90% diagnostic accuracy with no safety interventions required, demonstrating clinical viability.

The key unresolved question at the frontier of this lineage:
The AMIE prospective study demonstrates feasibility in text-based pre-visit consultations, but real-world clinical AI requires multimodal interaction (interpreting physical signs, reviewing imaging alongside conversation), longitudinal patient relationship management, and integration with electronic health records and clinical workflows. What architectural and training paradigm would enable a medical foundation model to serve as a continuous, multimodal clinical companion that maintains coherent longitudinal patient understanding across encounters while operating safely within existing healthcare systems?

Based on the lineage history, source-to-new-work changes, and the current frontier question, generate a new research idea as the next evolutionary node.

Return ONLY valid JSON. Do not wrap it in Markdown fences.
